opp.ais_why:
• On the 65-minute 1/16/2026 demo, BitSight produced 6 action items. The single AI-adjacent item is Amy Snider's internal-facing commitment to check with the legal team whether they currently use a CLM platform or AI-driven contract management. That is an internal inquiry, not a Zycus AI requirement, and pairs with BitSight's self-rated AI need on the RFP at the lowest non-zero tier, Basic Automation, rule based.
• The other action items are demo logistics: Ritika to open contract dashboards for visibility, Amy to review all vendor demos this month and shortlist by the third week of February, and Michael McCarthy to prepare a next demo scripted around the customer's actual contract data. The evaluation is being driven by demo quality and dashboard visibility, not AI capability.
• The most recent 5/11/2026 rep note records Global Director of Procurement Amy Snider confirming confidence in demos seen so far, with selection driven by demo quality. Products in scope are iContract, iSupplier, eInvoicing, and Merlin only on the AI side. Transcript keyword review confirmed no additional buyer-voiced AI engagement beyond the legal-internal-check, lifting confidence to High.

metrics.narrative: The business problem is clear and qualitative but never quantified. On the 14 Jan 2026 discovery call Amy Snider named GEP platform reliability as the single decisive issue: asked if one thing could be meaningfully better it would be reliability, she said 'Yeah. Absolutely. Reliability in the platform and in the product for sure.' She described 'constant bugs and glitches that we are dealing with every single week' forcing repeated support tickets. She also wants auto-PO creation from executed contracts to remove manual entry, a contract-renewal dashboard to replace Excel tracking, and a self-service supplier-onboarding intake she can edit without vendor help. None of this is tied to a dollar, headcount, or cycle-time figure, so there is no ROI model. Quantifying the cost of the reliability problem and the savings from automation is the move.

sources: SF contact role: Amy Snider, Decision Maker
champion.narrative: Amy Snider is a developing, well-engaged coach but not yet a true switch-driving champion. Positives: she actively shaped both calls, articulated pain candidly, signalled openness to changing processes ('I'm not married to anything if it doesn't make sense'), and ran the original 2022 GEP selection so she has internal procurement authority and credibility. What is missing: she is single-threaded (Bernardo Silva is the only other procurement person, invited but absent on 14 Jan), she has not introduced an Economic Buyer or assembled the broader team she promised, and she carries the political weight of having selected GEP herself, so reversing it is awkward for her. Her engagement has cooled into a rep-initiated holding pattern since 16 Jan. The conversion play is to give her the reliability proof and a quantified case so she can sell the switch internally — and to ask her, directly, whether the new CEO keeps this on the agenda.

competition.narrative: GEP Worldwide is the entrenched incumbent and the deal to beat: BitSight selected it ~4 years ago (2022), it is integrated with NetSuite, has strong user adoption ('very small' rogue spend), and runs the contract, supplier, PO, and eInvoicing stack today. The buyer's frustration is reliability, not function ('the actual system process is working well... the platform's just not stable enough'), so the competitive wedge is stability plus AI-driven usability (Merlin Slack intake, auto-PO, dashboards). Coupa, iValua, JAGGAER, and SAP Ariba are listed on the Salesforce competitor field as the broader shortlist but none surfaced by name in the read calls, so they are lower current threats than the incumbent. The 29 Jun next step frames the live battle precisely: differentiate from GEP on service and support quality. But note the real near-term threat is not a competitor at all — it is the new CEO deprioritising the project and the status quo winning by inertia.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is specific, confirmed, and owned by Amy Snider's procurement team. Primary pain: GEP platform instability, 'constant bugs and glitches every single week' generating repeated support tickets, which Amy says is 'not a normal thing' for a mature platform. Operational pain: she tracks 15+ monthly contract renewals on Excel because GEP has no renewal dashboard; she and Bernardo must manually tag each other on contracts to share visibility; PO creation after a contract executes is fully manual; and intake-question wording changes require waiting on the vendor. She uses ChatGPT outside the system to compare renewal clauses, signalling unmet AI need. The pain owner is the two-person procurement team (Amy and Bernardo Silva). Inferred but not confirmed: downstream AP and legal friction.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are unknown after ~5.5 months. There is no discussion of MSA/SOW, redlines, procurement channel, or InfoSec for a Zycus purchase. GEP is integrated with NetSuite (BitSight's ERP) and POs live in GEP, so any switch implies an integration/migration scope that has not been touched. The incumbent's contract status and any renewal/expiry forcing function are not on record. The new-CEO transition means even a willing buyer cannot run paper until leadership settles, so paper is not the live constraint yet — re-qualification is. Capturing the GEP contract expiry would still surface any natural compelling event.

sources: SF account contacts: James Hillier (CFO), Dave Casion (CTO)
economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer is engaged. Amy Snider is the Global Director of Procurement and the only buyer-side voice across both transcribed calls, and on a $130k platform replacement a Director cannot sign alone. CFO James Hillier and CTO Dave Casion exist as account contacts but have never appeared on a call. Amy did reference executive backing for the original GEP rollout ('We have that support, on the executive side as well that that pushed that'), which signals an exec layer exists above her but has not been brought into this evaluation. The 29 Jun next step confirms the most senior signal is now an incoming new CEO whose arrival has paused the decision. The path to power here runs through Amy, not around her: she authored the GEP frustration and ran the 2022 selection, so arming her to broker the new-exec conversation is the correct play, not cold-outreach.

decision_process.narrative: The buyer's intended process was stated on 14 Jan: run the Friday demo, then Amy down-selects vendors and brings in the broader team for the next evaluation stage. The call sequence confirms the early stage: an intro on 7 Jan, discovery on 14 Jan, demo on 16 Jan, all single-threaded to Amy (Bernardo Silva was invited but could not attend). The process has since stalled; the 29 Jun next step records that the final decision is delayed because a new CEO is coming onboard, which inserts a leadership-approval dependency that did not exist before. The broader cross-functional team (legal, GRC, AP, IT/business systems) that Amy said she would assemble has not engaged in evidence. The first job is to re-confirm with Amy whether this evaluation survives the leadership change at all, then map the post-CEO approval chain.

decision_criteria.narrative: The criteria are well evidenced from the 14 Jan discovery and the 16 Jan demo. The number-one criterion is platform reliability and stability, explicitly ranked above everything else by Amy Snider ('if one thing could be meaningfully better... reliability... Absolutely'). Secondary criteria are user-friendliness and self-service configurability (editing intake questions without the vendor), a contract-renewal dashboard with visual status, a shared purchasing user role so she and Bernardo can both see all contracts, auto-PO creation from signed contracts, no loss of current eInvoicing functionality (suppliers self-uploading invoices), and Slack-based intake via Merlin. On the demo she also probed reporting, clause comparison against BitSight's MSA, negotiated-savings fields, and Slack approvals. Risk review is explicitly out of scope for now (BitSight runs TPRM on its own product). The wedge versus the GEP incumbent is reliability plus AI-driven user adoption.

items.detail: New CEO arrival (29 Jun Next Step note) delays final decision with no new timeline. New execs typically freeze or reset vendor evaluations to avoid early-tenure risk. P2P platform investment may be deprioritized or re-evaluated under new leadership. This, not a competitor, is the deal's top near-term threat.

GEP is the 2022-selected incumbent with 4 years of entrenchment, executive support, strong user adoption, and NetSuite integration. Amy led that selection and now must justify reversing it. Switching cost is high and explicitly cited by Amy ('bearing the cost and the risk'). No exec sponsor or coalition to champion the change.

No Economic Buyer identified. No CFO, COO, or exec sponsor engagement. Amy (Director-level) is sole contact and does not own budget authority. CEO transition (29 Jun) may have reset approval chain. For $130k P2P platform replacement, exec sign-off is required and is not secured.

Close date 29 Jul (29 days) is indefensible. Last buyer engagement 16 Jan (~165 days ago). No broader team, no EB, no contract/SOW, no legal/InfoSec scoping. Shortlisted to close in 29 days with a paused decision is impossible. No post-CEO timeline exists.

Reliability proof gap. Reliability is the #1 decision criterion ('if one thing could be meaningfully better, would it be reliability? Yeah. Absolutely.'). GEP's instability is the pain ('constant bugs and glitches every single week'). Yet no Zycus uptime SLA, no customer reference on stability, no technical architecture review, and no head-to-head reliability comparison has been provided. Without this proof, the switching-cost bar is not cleared.

No quantified business case: GEP instability pain confirmed but no downtime hours, ticket volume, productivity loss, or ROI calculated. Without quantified impact, cannot justify $130k switching cost to a CFO or a new CEO.

GEP has a 4-year user base, strong adoption ('very small rogue spend'), and an exec-supported 2022 rollout. Switching requires re-training procurement, AP, legal, GRC, and end users. No implementation plan, change-management strategy, or disruption-mitigation approach has been scoped with Amy or IT.
GEP is integrated with NetSuite (BitSight's ERP). No Zycus-NetSuite integration scoping, no IT/business-systems engagement, and no migration/cutover plan discussed after 173 days.
